title: Building Games with Phaser
text:
Preface
This book walks through building 2D games with Phaser, a fast and free open-source framework for browser-based games. We use Phaser 4 with Vite as our build tool.
Each chapter builds on the last, starting from project setup and working toward a complete playable game.

title: Building Games with Phaser
text:
Prerequisites

Basic familiarity with JavaScript
Node.js installed
A code editor (VS Code recommended)

title: Building Games with Phaser
section: How to Run the Game Locally
text:
How to Run the Game Locally
npm install       # first time only
npm run dev       # starts dev server at localhost:5174
Open the URL in your browser. Any file you edit and save will be reflected on reload.

title: 1  Getting Started with Phaser
text: 1.1 The Phaser Library

title: 1  Getting Started with Phaser
text:
1.1.1 What Phaser Is
Phaser is an open-source HTML5 game framework for building 2D games that run in the browser. It handles everything a game needs below the level of your own logic: rendering, physics, input, audio, asset loading, animation, cameras, tilemaps, and the game loop itself. You write the game rules; Phaser handles the machinery.
Phaser renders to an HTML <canvas> element using either WebGL (GPU-accelerated, default when available) or Canvas 2D (CPU-based fallback). On modern hardware, WebGL enables thousands of sprites at 60 fps with effects like lighting, shaders, and post-processing that would be impossible in Canvas 2D.


1.1.2 What Kinds of Games You Can Build
Phaser is a general-purpose 2D game framework. It has been used to build:







Genre
Examples




Platformers
Side-scrolling jumpers, Metroidvanias


Top-down games
RPGs, twin-stick shooters, Zelda-style adventures


Puzzle games
Match-3, block puzzles, physics puzzlers


Shoot-em-ups
Vertical and horizontal bullet-hell games


Idle / clicker games
Resource management, incremental games


Card games
Turn-based games with drag-and-drop mechanics


Tilemapped worlds
Large scrolling maps built from reusable tile grids


Visual novels
Dialogue trees, cutscenes, branching narratives



What Phaser is not suited for: 3D games (use Three.js or Babylon.js), very large MMOs, or applications that are more app than game. It is purpose-built for 2D interactive experiences delivered in the browser.


1.1.3 Phaser’s Major Subsystems
Phaser is organized into subsystems — each one responsible for a different aspect of the game. You access them through the scene as this.<system>. Here are the ones most relevant to a platformer:

The Scene System
A Scene is the fundamental unit of organization in Phaser. Every screen in your game — the title screen, the game level, the pause menu, the game-over screen — is a separate scene.
Each scene has three lifecycle methods you override:
preload()  // load assets (images, audio, tilemaps) before the scene runs
create()   // set up game objects once when the scene starts
update()   // called every frame (~60 times/second) — game logic goes here
Our game currently has one scene (GameScene). In a larger game you might have:
BootScene      → loads a loading bar graphic
PreloadScene   → loads all game assets while showing the loading bar
MenuScene      → title screen with Play button
GameScene      → the actual level
PauseScene     → overlay rendered on top of GameScene
GameOverScene  → shown when the player dies
Scenes can run simultaneously (e.g. a HUD scene layered over the game scene), be paused, resumed, put to sleep, or shut down entirely. The scene manager (this.scene) controls all of this.


Physics — Arcade
this.physics is the arcade physics system. It gives game objects a body — a rectangle or circle that participates in the physics simulation each frame.
There are two kinds of physics bodies:

Dynamic — affected by gravity, velocity, and collisions. The player is dynamic.
Static — fixed in place, immovable. Platforms and the ground are static.

Key arcade physics concepts used in the platformer:
// Create a dynamic physics sprite
this.player = this.physics.add.sprite(x, y, 'key');

// Create a static group (platforms)
this.platforms = this.physics.add.staticGroup();

// Prevent the player from leaving the canvas
this.player.setCollideWorldBounds(true);

// Make player land on platforms
this.physics.add.collider(this.player, this.platforms);

// Detect when player touches a coin (no solid collision — just detection)
this.physics.add.overlap(this.player, this.coins, callback);

// Read velocity and blocked state in update()
this.player.body.setVelocityX(-220);
this.player.body.setVelocityY(-550);  // jump
this.player.body.blocked.down;        // true when standing on something
The difference between collider and overlap is important:

collider — objects physically push each other apart (player lands on a platform)
overlap — detects contact without any physical response (player touches a coin)



Game Objects
Everything visible in the scene is a game object. The main types:








Type
Created via
Use case




Sprite
this.physics.add.sprite()
Animated characters, enemies


Image
this.physics.add.staticImage()
Non-animated objects (ground)


Text
this.add.text()
Score display, labels, UI


Graphics
this.make.graphics()
Drawing shapes procedurally


Group
this.physics.add.staticGroup()
Managing collections of objects


TilemapLayer
map.createLayer()
Tile-based level geometry



In our game, all textures are generated procedurally via this.make.graphics() so no image files are needed. This is the genRect() and genCircle() helper approach in GameScene.js.


Input
this.input manages all input devices. For a platformer, keyboard input is the main concern:
// Arrow keys + Space (built-in shortcut)
this.cursors = this.input.keyboard.createCursorKeys();

// Custom keys (WASD)
this.wasd = this.input.keyboard.addKeys({
  left:  Phaser.Input.Keyboard.KeyCodes.A,
  right: Phaser.Input.Keyboard.KeyCodes.D,
  jump:  Phaser.Input.Keyboard.KeyCodes.W
});
Two ways to read a key:

key.isDown — true on every frame the key is held. Use for continuous actions like moving left/right.
Phaser.Input.Keyboard.JustDown(key) — true only on the single frame the key is first pressed. Use for one-shot actions like jumping (prevents holding the jump key from triggering multiple jumps).



The Camera
this.cameras.main is the default camera. For a small fixed-screen game like ours, the camera never moves. But in a scrolling platformer, you bind it to follow the player:
this.cameras.main.startFollow(this.player);
this.cameras.main.setBounds(0, 0, worldWidth, worldHeight);
The camera crops and offsets what is drawn each frame, allowing a world much larger than the canvas.


Asset Loading
preload() is where you load external assets before the scene runs. Phaser queues all load calls and shows a built-in loading bar, then fires create() only after everything is ready:
preload() {
  this.load.image('sky', 'assets/sky.png');
  this.load.spritesheet('player', 'assets/dude.png', {
    frameWidth: 32, frameHeight: 48
  });
  this.load.audio('jump', 'assets/jump.mp3');
  this.load.tilemapTiledJSON('map', 'assets/level1.json');
}
Our current game skips preload() entirely because all textures are generated programmatically. This is useful for prototyping — no asset pipeline needed.


Animations
this.anims manages sprite animations. An animation is a sequence of frames from a spritesheet played at a given frame rate:
// Define once in create()
this.anims.create({
  key: 'run',
  frames: this.anims.generateFrameNumbers('player', { start: 0, end: 3 }),
  frameRate: 12,
  repeat: -1       // -1 = loop forever
});

// Play it on a sprite
this.player.play('run');
this.player.play('idle');
Our current game has no animations because the player is a plain rectangle. Adding a spritesheet and animations is the natural next step.


Audio
this.sound plays audio files loaded in preload():
// One-shot sound effect
this.sound.play('jump');

// Looping background music
this.bgMusic = this.sound.add('music', { loop: true, volume: 0.5 });
this.bgMusic.play();


Quiz: Phaser Subsystems
Which of the following is handled by this.input, NOT by this.physics?

 Resolving collisions between the player and platforms  Applying gravity to dynamic bodies  Reading which keyboard keys are currently pressed  Detecting overlap between the player and coins Check Answer







1.1.4 How Phaser Is Structured as a Package
When you run npm install, Phaser is downloaded into node_modules/phaser/. The library is one large bundle that includes all its subsystems. You import it in main.js as:
import * as Phaser from 'phaser';
Vite then performs tree-shaking at build time — it analyzes which parts of Phaser your code actually uses and strips out the rest. This keeps the final dist/ bundle smaller than the full library.
The ^4.0.0 version in package.json means Phaser 4, which was released in 2024. Phaser 4 is a significant rewrite of Phaser 3, with a new renderer architecture and updated APIs. Most online tutorials and documentation target Phaser 3; be aware of version differences when reading external resources.

title: 1  Getting Started with Phaser
text:
1.2 The Local Development Workflow
Because we use Vite’s dev server (npm run dev), there is no build step during development:

Edit any file in VS Code and save
Reload the browser tab
Changes are live immediately

Vite serves your files directly from disk in dev mode — there is no dist/ folder until you run npm run build. The dist/ folder only exists for deployment.






Tipnpm run dev vs npm run build











Command
What it does
When to use




npm run dev
Starts a local dev server (e.g. localhost:5174). No output folder. Hot-reload friendly.
During development


npm run build
Bundles and minifies everything into dist/. No server.
Before deploying

title: 1  Getting Started with Phaser
text:
1.3 Project Structure and Bootstrapping Phaser
Every Phaser project built with Vite has the same three core files. Understanding what each one does — and how they connect — is the foundation for everything that follows.
phaser/
├── index.html      ← shell page loaded by the browser
├── src/
│   ├── main.js     ← configures and starts the Phaser engine
│   └── GameScene.js ← all game logic lives here
└── package.json    ← Vite + Phaser dependencies

1.3.1 index.html — The Shell
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8" />
  <title>Phaser Starter</title>
  <style>
    * { margin: 0; padding: 0; box-sizing: border-box; }
    body { background: #2e251a; display: flex; justify-content: center;
           align-items: center; height: 100vh; }
  </style>
</head>
<body>
  <script type="module" src="/src/main.js"></script>
</body>
</html>
index.html is nearly empty by design. Its only real job is this one line:
<script type="module" src="/src/main.js"></script>
This tells the browser to load main.js as a JavaScript module, which kicks off the entire game. The CSS centers the canvas and sets the page background color so the game sits flush in the middle of the screen.






Note



type="module" is required here because main.js uses ES module import syntax. Without it, the browser would throw a syntax error on the import statement.




1.3.2 main.js — Configuring and Starting Phaser
import * as Phaser from 'phaser';
import GameScene from './GameScene.js';

const config = {
  type: Phaser.AUTO,
  width: 800,
  height: 500,
  backgroundColor: '#2d2d44',
  physics: {
    default: 'arcade',
    arcade: { gravity: { y: 600 }, debug: false }
  },
  scene: [GameScene]
};

new Phaser.Game(config);
config is a plain JavaScript object that describes how the game should be set up. It is passed directly to new Phaser.Game().








Key
Value
What it does




type
Phaser.AUTO
Let Phaser choose WebGL or Canvas based on what the browser supports. You can force one with Phaser.WEBGL or Phaser.CANVAS.


width / height
800 / 500
Canvas size in pixels.


backgroundColor
'#2d2d44'
Color painted behind all game objects.


physics.default
'arcade'
Which physics engine to use.


physics.arcade.gravity.y
600
Downward gravitational pull in units per second². Higher = heavier feel.


physics.arcade.debug
false
When true, draws collision boxes over every physics body. Useful during development.


scene
[GameScene]
The list of scenes to register. The first scene starts automatically.



Phaser ships with two physics engines:

arcade — Simple, fast, axis-aligned rectangles and circles. Ideal for most 2D games. Does not support rotation-based collisions.
matter — Full rigid-body physics via Matter.js. Supports rotation, complex polygon shapes, joints, and constraints. More realistic but computationally heavier.

new Phaser.Game(config) is what actually starts the game. When called, Phaser:

Creates the <canvas> element and injects it into the DOM
Initializes the renderer (WebGL or Canvas per type)
Sets up the physics engine
Starts the game loop (the update/render cycle that runs every frame)
Launches the first scene in the scene array — in this case, GameScene

Without it, config is just an inert object in memory.

Quiz: What new Phaser.Game(config) does
Before new Phaser.Game(config) runs, config is just a plain object sitting in memory. What does calling new Phaser.Game(config) actually cause to happen?

 It parses the config object and prints it to the console — nothing visible happens on screen.  It creates the <canvas>, initializes the renderer and physics engine, starts the game loop, and launches the first scene listed in config.scene.  It downloads Phaser from the network and installs it into node_modules/.  It registers the scenes but waits for an explicit game.start() call to begin rendering. Check Answer







1.3.3 package.json — Project Manifest
{
  "name": "phaser",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "",
  "main": "index.js",
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "vite",
    "build": "vite build"
  },
  "keywords": [],
  "author": "",
  "license": "ISC",
  "type": "commonjs",
  "dependencies": {
    "phaser": "^4.0.0"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "vite": "^8.0.8"
  }
}
package.json is the manifest file that Node.js and npm use to understand your project. Every JavaScript project that uses npm has one.
Metadata fields:








Field
Value
Purpose




name
"phaser"
The project name. Matters if you publish to npm; otherwise just a label.


version
"1.0.0"
Semantic version of your project (major.minor.patch).


description
""
Short description — empty here, but useful for published packages.


main
"index.js"
The entry point if this package were imported by another project. Not used directly by Vite, but kept for compatibility.


keywords
[]
Tags for npm search. Not relevant for a private game project.


author
""
Your name or organization.


license
"ISC"
The open-source license. ISC is a permissive license similar to MIT.



scripts are shortcuts you run with npm run <name>:

npm run dev — runs vite, starting the local dev server
npm run build — runs vite build, bundling the project into dist/

type: "commonjs" tells Node.js which module system to use for .js files. This governs the build process; the type="module" attribute in index.html is a separate, browser-side setting. Vite bridges the two automatically.
dependencies vs devDependencies:

dependencies — packages your game needs at runtime. Phaser belongs here because it runs in the browser alongside your game code.
devDependencies — packages only needed during development. Vite belongs here because once the game is built, the browser never sees it.

Version ranges: The caret (^) in "phaser": "^4.0.0" means accept any version >=4.0.0 and <5.0.0. When you run npm install, the exact versions installed are written into package-lock.json, which locks the environment for everyone on the project.


1.3.4 How the Three Files Connect
Browser loads index.html
    └── <script type="module"> loads main.js
            ├── imports Phaser from node_modules
            ├── imports GameScene from ./GameScene.js
            ├── defines config {}
            └── new Phaser.Game(config)
                    └── starts the game loop → runs GameScene
index.html delegates everything to main.js. main.js delegates everything to new Phaser.Game(). From that point on, Phaser takes over and calls into your scene’s create() and update() methods on every frame.

title: 1  Getting Started with Phaser
1  Getting Started with Phaser
text:
1.5 How the Browser Runs GameScene.js
Understanding what the browser is doing under the hood while your game runs changes how you think about every line of code in GameScene.js.

1.5.1 JavaScript is single-threaded
The browser’s JavaScript engine (V8 in Chrome, SpiderMonkey in Firefox) runs on a single thread. It processes one task at a time. There is no parallel execution — if two things need to happen, one waits for the other to finish. This is the fundamental constraint that governs everything below.


1.5.2 The event loop and the task queue
JavaScript in the browser is driven by an event loop. The loop continuously checks a queue of pending tasks and runs them one at a time. Tasks come from many sources: a timer firing, a network response arriving, a user clicking, or a frame being requested. The loop picks up the next task, runs it to completion, then picks up the next one.
Your game code does not run continuously — it runs in discrete tasks, each scheduled by the browser.


1.5.3 requestAnimationFrame — how Phaser hooks in
When new Phaser.Game(config) starts, Phaser calls requestAnimationFrame(loop), which tells the browser: “call this loop function right before you paint the next frame to the screen.” The browser calls it approximately 60 times per second, synchronized to the display’s refresh rate (60 Hz on most monitors). Each call is one tick — one iteration of the game loop.
After each tick completes, Phaser immediately schedules the next one with another requestAnimationFrame call. This creates the continuous loop:
rAF fires → tick runs → rAF fires again → tick runs → ...
Between ticks, the browser is free to handle other tasks: responding to a click, running a network callback, updating the DOM. Your game is not monopolizing the thread — it is sharing it politely, yielding between frames.


1.5.4 The order of operations inside one tick
Within a single tick, Phaser runs a fixed sequence of steps. The order matters:
1. Input sampling     — keyboard/mouse state is read and recorded
2. Physics step       — gravity applied, positions updated, collisions resolved
3. Overlap callbacks  — any registered overlap callbacks fire (e.g. coin collection)
4. Your update()      — your code in update() runs
5. Render             — all game objects are drawn to the canvas
This ordering has practical consequences:

Input is sampled before your code runs. By the time update() reads cursors.left.isDown, the keyboard state already reflects what is physically pressed at the start of this frame. You are never reading a stale value from the previous frame.
Overlap callbacks fire before update(). If the player touches a coin and the callback runs coin.destroy(), by the time your update() code executes in the same frame, the coin is already gone from the scene. You never observe a half-destroyed state.
Render happens last. No matter what velocities or positions you set in update(), none of it becomes visible until the render step. All changes made during a single tick appear simultaneously in the next frame — the player never sees an intermediate state.


Quiz: Order of operations per tick
Within a single tick of Phaser’s game loop, what is the order in which these steps execute?

 Render → Your update() → Physics step → Input sampling  Input sampling → Physics step (including overlap callbacks) → Your update() → Render  Your update() → Input sampling → Physics step → Render  Physics step → Render → Input sampling → Your update() Check Answer







1.5.5 Priorities and what “listening” means
In the context of your game, “listening” means Phaser has registered a callback that it will invoke at the right moment in the tick sequence. When you write:
this.physics.add.overlap(this.player, this.coins, (_player, coin) => {
  coin.destroy();
  this.score++;
  this.scoreText.setText('Score: ' + this.score);
});
You are not asking Phaser to check overlaps immediately. You are registering a callback that Phaser stores internally and calls during the physics step of every subsequent tick — but only if an overlap is detected. Your callback code has no priority level of its own; its place in the sequence is determined by when in the tick Phaser chooses to invoke it (during the physics step, before your update()).
The same is true of colliders:
this.physics.add.collider(this.player, this.platforms);
This registers a collision handler that Phaser checks every tick. You do not poll it yourself — Phaser runs it automatically as part of the physics step.


1.5.6 create() runs outside the loop
create() is special: it runs exactly once, synchronously, before the first tick of the game loop begins. This means:

No physics is running while create() executes
No frames are being rendered
The entire scene setup — textures, objects, colliders, input bindings — completes in a single uninterrupted task

This is why it is safe to write sequential setup code in create() without worrying about race conditions. Everything runs in order, top to bottom, before the game loop ever starts.


1.5.7 Frame rate and delta
Phaser passes time and delta as arguments to update(time, delta). delta is the number of milliseconds since the last frame — normally around 16.7 ms at 60 fps, but it can be longer if the browser was busy with something else. Our current game ignores delta, which means movement speed is frame-rate dependent (slightly faster on a 144 Hz monitor than a 60 Hz one). A more robust approach multiplies velocities by delta / 1000 to make speed measured in pixels per second, independent of frame rate.


1.5.8 What this means for your code
The practical lesson: everything in update() is code that runs 60 times per second, so keep it lean. Creating new objects, allocating arrays, or doing heavy calculations inside update() will cause frame drops. Setup work belongs in create(). The update loop is for reading state and setting velocities — not for construction.
The browser will not warn you if update() takes too long. It will simply drop frames silently, and the game will feel choppy. A frame that takes longer than ~16.7 ms to complete misses its render window and the player sees a stutter.

title: 2  Enemies, Winning, and Losing
text:
2.1 Play it
In Chapter 1 the world was peaceful: you wandered around, collected coins, and nothing ever stopped you. Chapter 2 gives the game stakes — an enemy that can kill the player, a clear win condition (collect every coin), and a way to restart after the game ends.
Along the way, four new ideas enter the codebase:
Everything from Chapter 1 still applies. The structure of GameScene.js is unchanged: a class with constructor, create, update, and helpers. We are adding one new method (_endGame) and extending the existing ones.
The live game is embedded below. Collect all five coins to win. Touch the red enemy patrolling the middle platform and it’s game over. Press R at any time to restart.

title: 2  Enemies, Winning, and Losing
2  Enemies, Winning, and Losing
text:
2.3 What changed from Chapter 1
At a glance, the diff from Chapter 1 is:

A new enemy texture — a red 28×28 square.
A new enemy sprite that patrols via a tween.
A tween in place of manual movement in update().
A win condition tracked by this.totalCoins.
A lose condition triggered by overlapping the enemy.
A this.gameOver flag that short-circuits update() and the coin callback when the game is over.
An R key bound in create() and checked at the top of update().
A new _endGame method that pauses physics, kills tweens, and renders an overlay.
The hint text now mentions R to restart.

Everything else — the ground, platforms, player, coins, colliders, score — is identical to Chapter 1. The rest of this chapter walks through each addition.

title: 2  Enemies, Winning, and Losing
text:
2.4 The enemy

2.4.1 Texture
this.genRect('enemy', 28, 28, 0xff4444);
Same pattern as every other texture in Chapter 1: a named 28×28 red square registered in the texture cache. 0xff4444 is a soft red — not pure 0xff0000, which would be too harsh against the dark background.


2.4.2 Sprite body configuration
this.enemy = this.physics.add.image(440, 227, 'enemy');
this.enemy.body.setAllowGravity(false);
this.enemy.body.setImmovable(true);
Two deliberate choices here.
physics.add.image — not physics.add.sprite. sprite is used for objects that play animations; image is for static textures. The enemy never animates (the tween moves its position, not its texture), so image is the lighter-weight choice.
setAllowGravity(false) — by default every dynamic physics body is pulled down by gravity each frame. If we left gravity on, the enemy would fall off the platform immediately. Turning gravity off makes the enemy behave like it is floating in place, which is what we want for a platform-bound patrol.
setImmovable(true) — prevents the enemy from being pushed around by collisions. We are not using a collider between the enemy and the player (we use an overlap), so this isn’t strictly required in the current code, but it’s a useful default: it makes the enemy behave like a fixed hazard rather than something the player can bump into and nudge.
The position (440, 227) places the enemy just above the middle platform. The platform sits at y = 250 and is 18 pixels tall (so its top is at y = 241); the enemy is 28 pixels tall, so centering it at y = 227 puts its bottom at exactly 241 — resting perfectly on the platform surface.


2.4.3 The tween
this.tweens.add({
  targets: this.enemy,
  x: 560,
  duration: 1400,
  yoyo: true,
  repeat: -1,
  ease: 'Sine.easeInOut'
});
A tween is Phaser’s way of animating any numeric property of any game object over time. You specify the target(s), the property, the destination value, how long it should take, and an easing curve — Phaser does the rest.
Each field:








Field
Value
Meaning




targets
this.enemy
Which object(s) to animate. Can be a single object or an array.


x: 560
destination
The property and target value. The enemy starts at x = 440 and the tween moves it to x = 560.


duration
1400 ms
How long one leg of the tween takes.


yoyo
true
When the destination is reached, play the tween in reverse back to the start.


repeat
-1
-1 means loop forever. Any positive number is the repeat count.


ease
'Sine.easeInOut'
The easing curve. Sine.easeInOut accelerates at the start and decelerates at the end, producing smooth back-and-forth motion rather than a mechanical bounce.



Without yoyo, the enemy would reach x = 560 and snap back instantly to x = 440 to restart. With yoyo, Phaser animates it smoothly back.
Tweens are scheduled once and run autonomously on a separate internal clock. You do not write this.enemy.x = ... in update() — the tween system does that for you each frame. This is the difference between declarative motion (tween: “move from here to there over 1.4s”) and imperative motion (update: “every frame, add 2 to my x position”).


2.4.4 Overlap for the lose condition
this.physics.add.overlap(this.player, this.enemy, () => {
  this._endGame('Game Over', 0xb71c1c);
});
Same pattern as the coin overlap from Chapter 1. When the player’s body and the enemy’s body overlap, Phaser calls the callback. Unlike coins, the enemy is not destroyed on contact — there is no .destroy(). It just ends the game.






NoteWhy overlap, not collider?



A collider would physically push the player away from the enemy, which feels wrong for a hazard. An overlap detects contact without any physical response, letting the _endGame call take over immediately. The player passes right through the enemy — because in the same frame we pause physics, so “passing through” is never observed.

title: 2  Enemies, Winning, and Losing
2  Enemies, Winning, and Losing
text:
2.6 The gameOver flag
this.score = 0;
this.gameOver = false;
Initialized at the bottom of create(), alongside this.score. Both are plain JavaScript properties on the scene instance. They exist for the lifetime of the scene — which is to say, until the scene is shut down or restarted.
The flag has three jobs:

Prevent _endGame from running twice. If the player somehow triggers both the win and lose conditions in the same frame, only the first one has any effect.
Suppress player input after the game ends. Checked at the top of update().
Stop coin pickups from continuing after the game ends. Checked at the top of the coin overlap callback.

This is a very common pattern in game code: a single boolean flag that expresses a state transition. Entering the “game over” state is as simple as setting the flag; every other piece of code that needs to behave differently just reads it.

title: 2  Enemies, Winning, and Losing
2  Enemies, Winning, and Losing
2  Enemies, Winning, and Losing
2  Enemies, Winning, and Losing
text:
2.10 Summary
Chapter 2 introduces the minimum machinery needed to turn a sandbox into a game: a hazard, a goal, and a reset button.

Tweens handle the enemy’s motion declaratively — targets / property / duration / ease — rather than you having to write position updates in update() every frame.
Scene-level state — the gameOver boolean — lets different parts of the code coordinate their behavior through a single shared flag.
Overlaps vs colliders — the enemy uses overlap because a hazard should not physically bounce the player; it should simply register contact and let game logic take over.
scene.restart() is the simplest possible reset: tear down everything, rebuild from scratch, start over.
Depth layering — setDepth(n) on the overlay ensures the end-game UI draws on top of the frozen game state, without disturbing the objects below.

In the next chapter we’ll move beyond procedurally-generated rectangles and introduce real sprite art with spritesheets and animations — so the blue box becomes a running, jumping character and the red square becomes something that actually looks threatening.

title: Appendix A: JavaScript Primer
text:
A.1 Values and Types
The game in this book is written entirely in JavaScript. If you’ve programmed before — in Python, Java, C++, or another language — most of the concepts will feel familiar. If JavaScript is your first language, this appendix walks through everything the game actually uses, in the order you’ll need it.
Every concept here connects directly to code from Chapter 1 or Chapter 2. Think of it as a reference: read it before you start, or come back to a specific section when something in the source code isn’t clicking.
JavaScript works with several kinds of values. The game uses three of them constantly.

title: Appendix A: JavaScript Primer
text:
Numbers
JavaScript has a single number type — it handles both integers and decimals.
800          // canvas width
-220         // velocity (negative = moving left)
0.55         // overlay opacity (a decimal between 0 and 1)
One number syntax that appears throughout the game is hexadecimal — base-16 notation for colors. In JavaScript, hexadecimal literals are written with a 0x prefix:
0x4caf50     // medium green (ground)
0xff4444     // soft red (enemy)
0xffd700     // gold (coin)
These represent the same colors as #4caf50 in CSS — just written differently. The 0x prefix tells JavaScript to interpret the digits in base 16, where a=10, b=11, c=12, d=13, e=14, f=15. So 0xff = 255, the maximum value per color channel.


Strings
A string is a sequence of characters, written between single quotes, double quotes, or backticks. The game uses single quotes throughout.
'Score: 0'          // text to display
'#ffffff'           // CSS-style color string (for Phaser text objects)
'monospace'         // font family name
'GameScene'         // scene key
The game builds strings by concatenation — joining them with +:
this.scoreText.setText('Score: ' + this.score);
// If this.score is 3, this produces the string 'Score: 3'
When you add a string and a number with +, JavaScript converts the number to a string and joins them. 'Score: ' + 3 gives 'Score: 3', not an error.


Booleans
A boolean is simply true or false. Booleans appear wherever the game needs to track a yes/no state:
this.gameOver = false;    // game is running
debug: false              // physics debug mode off
yoyo: true                // tween reverses when it reaches its target
Boolean values also appear as the result of comparisons and conditions:
const onFloor = body.blocked.down;
// blocked.down is true when the player is resting on a surface

Quiz: Hex color literals
The game uses 0xff4444 for the enemy’s red color. What decimal number does 0xff4444 equal — and why does that matter?

 16,724,036, but it doesn’t matter — the 0x prefix tells JavaScript to treat it as a string color name.  16,728,132 — a regular integer. Phaser accepts it because it encodes the red/green/blue bytes as 0xRRGGBB (ff / 44 / 44), which is the same bit layout CSS uses when you write #ff4444.  It is not a number — 0x values are JavaScript strings, and Phaser parses them internally.  65,535 — the 0xff4444 notation is truncated to two bytes. Check Answer

title: Appendix A: JavaScript Primer
text:
A.2 Variables: const and let
JavaScript declares variables with const or let. (There is a third keyword, var, which you’ll see in older code — avoid it in new code; it has confusing scoping behavior.)
const W = this.scale.width;   // W cannot be reassigned
let count = 0;                // count can be reassigned later
const means the binding cannot be reassigned after its initial value. It does not mean the value is frozen — you can still call methods on it and change its properties.
const player = this.physics.add.sprite(100, 420, 'player');
player.setCollideWorldBounds(true);   // fine — calling a method, not reassigning
player = somethingElse;              // TypeError — reassignment is not allowed
let allows reassignment:
let score = 0;
score++;         // score is now 1 — this works
score = 10;      // also fine
In the game, almost everything is const. The only values that need reassignment are counters and flags — and those live on this rather than as local variables, so they don’t use let directly either.






TipRule of thumb



Default to const. Switch to let only when you know you will reassign the variable. Using const everywhere it fits makes it easy to spot, at a glance, which variables are mutation points.



Quiz: What const actually guarantees
Given const player = this.physics.add.sprite(100, 420, 'player');, which of the following statements will throw a TypeError?

 player.setCollideWorldBounds(true);  player.x = 200;  player = this.physics.add.sprite(0, 0, 'enemy');  player.body.setVelocityX(-220); Check Answer

title: Appendix A: JavaScript Primer
Appendix A: JavaScript Primer
text:
A.4 Arrays
An array is an ordered list of values, written with [].
The game uses arrays in one key place:
scene: [GameScene]
This tells Phaser which scene classes to register. The first element starts automatically. A multi-scene game might look like:
scene: [BootScene, MenuScene, GameScene, GameOverScene]
Array elements are accessed by zero-based index:
const scenes = [BootScene, MenuScene, GameScene];
scenes[0]   // BootScene
scenes[2]   // GameScene
Arrays also appear inside Phaser’s tween API:
this.tweens.add({
  targets: this.enemy,   // could also be: targets: [enemy1, enemy2]
  ...
});

title: Appendix A: JavaScript Primer
Appendix A: JavaScript Primer
Appendix A: JavaScript Primer
Appendix A: JavaScript Primer
section: A.8 ES Modules: import and export
text:
A.8 ES Modules: import and export
Modern JavaScript is organized into modules — files that are isolated by default. Nothing defined in one file is visible to another unless explicitly shared.

export
To make something available to other files, you export it:
// GameScene.js
export default class GameScene extends Phaser.Scene { ... }
export default marks GameScene as the primary export of this file. A file can have only one default export. You can also have named exports (multiple per file):
export const VERSION = '2.0';           // named export
export function helper() { ... }        // named export
export default class GameScene { ... }  // default export


import
To use something from another file:
// main.js
import * as Phaser from 'phaser';
import GameScene from './GameScene.js';







Import form
What it does




import GameScene from './GameScene.js'
Imports the default export and names it GameScene


import * as Phaser from 'phaser'
Imports all exports as a namespace object called Phaser


import { Scene, AUTO } from 'phaser'
Imports specific named exports



The second form — import * as Phaser — is a namespace import. After it, you access everything through Phaser.Scene, Phaser.AUTO, Phaser.Input.Keyboard.KeyCodes.A, etc. The Phaser library exports dozens of named things; the namespace import brings them all in under one name without polluting the global scope.


Module file paths

'phaser' — a package name. Vite looks it up in node_modules/phaser/.
'./GameScene.js' — a relative path. ./ means “same directory as this file.”



type="module" in HTML
<script type="module" src="/src/main.js"></script>
This attribute tells the browser to treat main.js as an ES module, enabling import statements. Without it, the browser would throw a SyntaxError on the very first import line.

Quiz: Namespace imports
In main.js, the line import * as Phaser from 'phaser'; sets up which of the following?

 It imports only the default export from phaser and binds it to the name Phaser.  It imports every named export from phaser and groups them under a single namespace object called Phaser. Afterwards, Phaser.Scene, Phaser.AUTO, Phaser.Input.Keyboard.KeyCodes etc. all resolve through that namespace.  The * is a wildcard that tells Vite to re-download the Phaser library on every page load.  It adds everything in phaser to the browser’s global window object, so you can use Scene, AUTO, etc. anywhere. Check Answer
